step_by_step_thinking:
Blood on OT floor is cleaned by?
Clinical Reasoning:
The retrieved evidence specifically addresses environmental cleaning practices, stating that noncritical reusable items like toilets (OT floors) can be disinfected with low-level disinfectants such as sodium hypochlorite (bleach), quaternary ammonium compounds, phenolic disinfectants, or iodophors. Among these listed options, chlorine-based compounds (such as bleach/sodium hypochlorite) are explicitly referenced in multiple contexts related to decontamination of surfaces exposed to biological material—including blood spills—particularly due to their proven efficacy in killing bacteria and viruses. Although some options like phenol or quaternary ammonium compounds appear in the list, the direct reference to chlorine-based compounds in relation to blood spill cleanup is strongest and most directly supported by the text. Additionally, alcohol-based compounds are noted primarily for skin antisepsis and would degrade rapidly upon contact with blood and do not serve effectively as cleaners for large-scale organic spills. Thus, chlorine-based compounds are best suited given their documented performance against microbial contaminants found in blood.
